title: practical english usage - Part 316
content:
look forward to.

Before an object, we  use expect (with no preposition), hope for, wait for, and 5 prepositions hear from her.) I look forward to hearing from her. (= I feel pleasure at the thought that I will I'm waiting to hear from her. (= I need her email to come; perhaps it’ s late.) her to.) I hope to hear from her. (= I’ m  not sure whether she’ ll write, but I would like I expect to hear from her. (= I'm pretty sure I’ ll get an email from her.) Compare: 4 all  four    expressions         compared ‘ See you on Sunday.' ‘ I look forward to it.' He's looking forward to his birthday.

glad about.

One looks forward to something that seems certain to happen, and that one is Look forward means 'think about (something in the future) with pleasure’ .

3 look     forward:        meaning not Let's meet at the theatre. I'U be there at 6.00. (     I'll expect you at 6.00.) Come and see me this afternoon. I'll expect you at 4.00.

to our home, office, etc. Compare: When  we  say that we  expect a person, this usually means that he/she is coming I can't wait for the weekend!

Can't wait often expresses impatience.

I waited for her till eleven, and then went home.

- I expected her at ten, but she didn't turn up.

It's hard to wait for things when you're five years old.

- He expects to get a bike for his birthday. (= He thinks he’ ll get one.) not I hate waiting for people to phone, (     4-hate expecting people-to phone.) - I'm expecting a phone call from Jack at three o'clock. I hope he calls on time.

Word  Problems from A  to Z      Section 31 vocabulary' •  458  experiment and experience Sales person wanted - experience unnecessary.

doing things’ .

The  uncountable noun experience means 'the knowledge that you get from not (     H ave you ever experimented the feeling...?) Have you ever experienced the feeling that you were going mad?

(     I made a lot of interesting experiences .. .) I had a lot of interesting experiences during my year in Africa.

to experience.

in life. Experience is generally used with the verb have. There is also a verb An experience is something that you live through; something that happens to you I'm experimenting with a new way of organising my work.

gas from salt. (     We did an experience...) We did an experiment in the chemistry lesson, to see if you could get chlorine used with the verb do. There is also a verb to experiment.

prove something, or to see how  well something works. Experiment is generally An experiment is a test which  somebody does to see what the result will be, to 458  experime         nt   and   experience             ___ For and after wait, ►    99.   For wish, ►    632.

For hope and expect in negative clauses. ►    219.       For not and so after hope and expect, ►    585.

upset when I failed my exams.

My parents expected too much  of me when I was at school. They were terribly how  somebody ought to behave.

We can say that we  expect something of somebody, to express our feelings about Sarah isn't here. I expect she was too tired to come.

I expect you're all tired after your journey.

’ I suppose’ , 'ɪ have good reason to think’ .

I expect (that) can be used to talk about the present or past, with the meaning Staff are expected to start work punctually at 8.30.

Passive versions of the structure are also common.

We expect you to work on the first Saturday of every month.

Expect is often used with object + infinitive to talk about people’ s duties.

8  expect:       other     points I hope (that) she doesn't miss the train.

Hope (that) is often followed by a present tense with a future meaning (►  490).

I expect that we'll have a lot to talk about. (     I'm expecting that we'll...) Progressive forms of expect are not normally used before that-clauses.

NOT   I'm waiting that she arrives?

I expect (that) she'll be here soon. I hope (that) I'll recognise her.

Expect and hope can be used before a that-clause.

7  that-    cl  auses vocabulary •  460 fairly, quite, rather and pretty: adverbs of degree For word order rules and other details of the use of rather, ►    565.
